annotations.label: Principle of transfinite induction

anchor.snippet: The principle of transfinite induction is as follows:

annotations.note: `Ordinal.induction` states that if `p` holds at `j` whenever it holds for all `k < j`, then it holds for every ordinal, which is the principle of transfinite induction.

annotations.label: Induction by cases

anchor.snippet: A proof by transfinite induction is often broken down into three cases:

annotations.note: `Ordinal.limitRecOn` provides exactly the three-case induction principle (zero, successor, limit) used to prove properties of all ordinals.

annotations.label: Basis of a vector space via transfinite recursion

anchor.snippet: As an example, a basis for a (possibly infinite-dimensional) vector space can be created by starting with the empty set

annotations.note: `Basis.ofVectorSpace` produces a basis for an arbitrary vector space (the result of the construction), though Mathlib's proof uses Zorn's lemma rather than explicit transfinite recursion.

annotations.label: Transfinite Recursion Theorem (version 1)
annotations.note: `WellFoundedLT.fix` (and `IsWellFounded.fix`) yields the unique value at each point from a rule depending on all earlier values; applied to ordinals it gives the version-1 transfinite recursion theorem.

annotations.label: Transfinite Recursion Theorem (version 2)

anchor.snippet: Given a set g 1 , and class functions G 2 , G 3 , there exists a unique function

annotations.note: `Ordinal.limitRecOn` defines a function on ordinals by giving a zero value, a successor step, and a limit step, with computation rules `limitRecOn_zero/succ/limit` matching version 2.

annotations.label: Transfinite recursion on well-founded relations

anchor.snippet: More generally, one can define objects by transfinite recursion on any well-founded relation

annotations.note: `IsWellFounded.fix` defines values by recursion along any well-founded relation `r`, with `IsWellFounded.fix_eq` giving the defining equation.

anchor.snippet: The following construction of the Vitali set shows one way that the axiom of choice can be used

annotations.note: Grep for `Vitali set`/`VitaliSet` in Mathlib turns up only comments referencing such a set (in `MeasureTheory.Integral.Lebesgue.Basic`); no construction of a non-measurable Vitali set is formalized.
